explanation:
The statement describes that, within the conditions set by prop_0, if prop_1 and prop_2 (which is equivalent to prop_3) hold, and when prop_4 happens (which yields prop_5), then something about drop being positive applies if the pressure drops, but we don't have atomic propositions for 'drop' or 'pressure', so we only encode what's expressible.

Therefore, we are expressing: Always, if we are in prop_0, and (prop_1 and prop_2) holds, and prop_4 holds (which means prop_5 holds), then some unnamed property holds if the pressure drops, but we ignore that part and only encode the rest.

So, we encode: G ( prop_0 -> ( (prop_1 & prop_2) & prop_4 -> prop_5 ) ) .

Alternatively, if we take 'prop_3' as synonymous to 'prop_1 & prop_2', we can write: (prop_1 & prop_2) <-> prop_3, to reinforce the equivalence, but this depends on if you want to explicitly encode the equivalence.

Since 'pressure_drops' is not an allowed atomic proposition, we must use only the listed propositions. Based on the input, we interpret:
- prop_0 (i.e., prop_1) refers to one or both holding.
- prop_2 yields the condition prop_3 as 'if prop_2 then prop_3'.
- prop_4 if the pressure drops (but since we have no 'pressure_drops', we will take 'prop_4' as holding when intended by the system, without a direct trigger).
So, globally: if prop_2, then prop_3; globally: if prop_2, eventually (prop_0 or prop_1). prop_4's trigger cannot be stated without more information, so we only require prop_4 to eventually hold at some point (as the best safe translation).
output_LTL: G((dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV | dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV) -> (dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV)) & G((dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV | dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV) -> F((patientBreathingRequest | (breathingTime_greaterThanOrEqual_apneaLagTime & !failSafeMode)) | (inspiratoryPhase))) & F((dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV | dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV))

explanation: The statement describes that in PCV mode during the expiratory phase, within a trigger window, prop_0 and prop_1 become true (i.e., the system transitions to the inspiration phase) when prop_2 occurs (which is equivalent to saying it yields prop_3). This can be interpreted as: whenever prop_2 occurs within the expiratory phase, it causes prop_0 and prop_1 to become true, and prop_2 implies (or yields) prop_3. Since the context and boundaries ('expiration phase', 'trigger window') are not given as atomic propositions, we're limited to expressing the core logical relationship: whenever prop_2, then prop_0 and prop_1 and prop_3 will follow. This leads to: G (prop_2 -> (prop_0 & prop_1 & prop_3)).

Whenever prop_0 (drop(PAW) > ITS_PCV) holds within the trigger window during the expiratory phase (assumed context), then eventually prop_1 (goes in inspiration phase) should occur. This means that, each time prop_0 is true, prop_1 must become true at some future point. This is expressed by G(prop_0 -> F(prop_1)).

The statement seems to describe a sequence of events in a ventilator mode (PCV mode):
- 'Within the trigger window during the expiratory phase...'
- In the case of prop_1, prop_2 (i.e., if prop_3)
- when prop_4 (i.e., it yields the condition prop_5)
So, when prop_3 holds (so both prop_1 and prop_2), and prop_4 occurs, which yields prop_5. All of this occurs during a certain window (not further specified).
Translating this to LTL (using only LTL ops and APs):
If, at some point, (prop_3) and then prop_4 becomes true, and this leads to prop_5, then 'eventually' prop_4 -> prop_5 under the scope of prop_3 holding. Since the time window is not specified, I'll focus on the sequence:
Always if prop_3 holds, then always (prop_4 -> prop_5).

Whenever it is the expiratory phase during the trigger window, if there is spontaneous breathing and if prop_1 becomes true (i.e., the drop(PAW) > ITS_PCV condition is detected), then the ventilator shall prop_2 (i.e., perform prop_3). By the wording, prop_2 is equivalent to prop_3, so we can represent everything by prop_1 (condition), and prop_3 (the action). The statement is causal: 'if prop_1 then prop_3.' In LTL, this can be globally (in the relevant context), but since context ("expiratory phase, within the trigger window, in the case of spontaneous breathing") must be encoded as part of an atomic proposition (since we can't express temp. intervals or nonpropositional logic), we assume these are implicitly satisfied when the atomic props are considered. Thus, in LTL: globally, whenever prop_1 occurs, prop_3 must happen at the same time (the standard way to say this is G (prop_1 -> prop_3)).

The requirement describes that during the expiratory phase within the trigger window, if either prop_1 or prop_2 occurs (which implies prop_3), then, when prop_4 holds, this leads to condition prop_5. This can be captured by stating that globally, if we are in the expiration phase's trigger window and (prop_1 or prop_2 holds), then (prop_3 holds), and when prop_4 holds, prop_5 must also hold.

The statement is conditionally structured: 'Within prop_0, in the case of prop_1, the prop_2 (i.e., it prop_3) when it prop_4 (i.e., it yields the condition prop_5).' This can be translated as: whenever prop_0 holds, and prop_1 becomes true, then if prop_4 holds (and thus prop_5), then prop_2 (and thus prop_3).

The statement says that during the expiratory phase, when a spontaneous breath occurs within the trigger window, prop_0 should happen (which is, it prop_1), and this should occur when prop_2, which results in the condition prop_3. Translating this, it requires that whenever (spontaneous breathing within trigger window during expiratory phase) & (prop_2 holds), then eventually prop_0 happens, and that leads to prop_3 being true. Since the exact atomic propositions for 'spontaneous breath', 'trigger window', and 'expiratory phase' are not provided, we represent the core LTL relationship between the props as: if prop_2 occurs, then eventually prop_0 happens, and then prop_3 must hold. Thus, G (prop_2 -> F (prop_0 & X prop_3)).
output_LTL: G ((dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV | dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV) -> F ((patientBreathingRequest | dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV | dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV) & X (dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV)))

explanation: Interpretation: During the expiratory phase, within the trigger window, if prop_1 occurs, then prop_2 (i.e., prop_3) should happen when prop_4 occurs (which in turn yields the condition prop_5). This can be modeled as: whenever ((expiratory phase) & (trigger window) & prop_1), then globally, if prop_4 holds, then prop_2 (and hence prop_3) happens and it results in prop_5.
